profiles.headline: Principal MBSE lead for safety-critical aerospace programs
profiles.bio: Leads model-based systems engineering adoption across flight controls and mission-planning teams. Interested in verification rigor, requirements traceability, and making MBSE useful beyond slideware.

profiles.headline: Embedded systems architect bridging hardware, firmware, and test
profiles.bio: Works on cross-functional architecture for industrial robotics platforms. Cares about interface clarity, failure analysis, and disciplined system decomposition.

profiles.headline: Systems engineer for regulated medical device platforms
profiles.bio: Focuses on requirements, risk controls, and design history discipline for connected medtech products. Likes translating compliance pressure into cleaner engineering systems.

profiles.headline: Autonomy systems engineer working across perception, planning, and ops
profiles.bio: Helps autonomy teams reason about system boundaries, operational envelopes, and failure containment. Interested in reviewable architecture artifacts and simulation-informed decisions.

profiles.headline: Systems integration manager for large defense programs
profiles.bio: Coordinates subsystem integration across contractors, test teams, and program management. Particularly interested in configuration drift, interface governance, and review discipline.

profiles.headline: Systems safety engineer focused on hazard analysis and operational resilience
profiles.bio: Works with platform teams to turn safety cases into design decisions instead of compliance theater. Deeply interested in STPA, fault containment, and testable mitigations.

profiles.headline: Rail systems engineer coordinating infrastructure and rolling stock interfaces
profiles.bio: Works on large rail modernization efforts with a focus on interfaces, operational readiness, and test planning across vendors and public stakeholders.

profiles.headline: Digital thread strategist for complex product development organizations
profiles.bio: Helps teams connect requirements, models, verification evidence, and change control into a usable engineering thread. Suspicious of dashboards that do not improve decisions.

posts.title: What finally made MBSE reviews useful for our flight-controls team
posts.body: The biggest improvement was banning model tours. Reviews got better when every session had one operational question, one boundary condition, and one explicit decision to make. We also forced every diagram to point back to a requirement or verification artifact. The model became less decorative and more argumentative.

posts.title: A simple interface contract template cut our integration churn hard
posts.body: We kept rediscovering the same cross-team failures: assumed timings, undocumented reset behavior, and unclear ownership for degraded modes. A lightweight contract template fixed more than another architecture review ever did. We now require nominal flow, failure behavior, timing assumptions, and test ownership on every subsystem interface.

posts.title: Traceability gets easier once you stop pretending every requirement is equally important
posts.body: We had a messy requirement tree until we started separating safety-critical, regulatory, and convenience requirements much more explicitly. That made reviews sharper and verification planning less chaotic. The lesson: traceability quality comes from better requirement structure, not just more tooling.

posts.title: STPA was most useful for us when we paired it with interface ownership
posts.body: Hazard analysis alone did not change much. The big shift happened when every unsafe control action also had a named interface owner, mitigation owner, and test owner. That created accountability and turned safety analysis into engineering work instead of a PDF graveyard.

comments.post_title: What finally made MBSE reviews useful for our flight-controls team
comments.body: The requirement/verif link is the key. The moment the model has to support a decision, people stop stuffing it with ornamental detail.

comments.post_title: A simple interface contract template cut our integration churn hard
comments.body: We ended up doing something similar on a defense integration effort. The trick was forcing timing and degraded-mode assumptions into the same artifact instead of separate documents.
